aliases: Deep-fried lentil fritter with black beans

english: Deep-fried lentil fritter with black beans

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for vadai; 'fritter (food product)' is the closest defensible parent class for a deep-fried legume-based snack.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers deep-fried snack fritters; no specific vadai code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for vadai or Indian deep-fried legume fritter identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vadai with black beans is approximately 2 pieces (about 80g), commonly consumed as a snack or side dish at Indian hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vadai with kacang hitam — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Vadai with kacang hitam' (CR ID: F11090066). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — legume-based fritters and mixed legume dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vadai with black beans found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous legume-based fritter entries: urad dal (black gram) dishes and mixed legume preparations consistently show GI in the range 30–42 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The high dietary fibre (4.99g/100g) and legume base (urad dal + black beans) support a low-GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (17.51g × 0.80 serving fraction × 0.80 available carb factor) / 100 ≈ 9.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 651mg/100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid vadai with black beans due to its high sodium content of 651mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to one small piece and avoid pairing with high-sodium chutneys or sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35) and high dietary fibre (4.99g/100g) from legumes support glycaemic control, but deep-frying adds significant fat (12.82g/100g) requiring portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vadai with black beans has a low glycaemic index (~35) and high fibre from urad dal and black beans, making it a relatively favourable snack for blood glucose management when limited to 1–2 pieces; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate and fat intake and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (651mg/100g), total fat (12.82g/100g), and estimated trans fat (~0.1g/100g) from deep-frying are adverse cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume vadai with black beans only occasionally and in small portions due to its high sodium content (651mg/100g), significant total fat from deep-frying, and estimated trans fat (~0.1g/100g); baking or air-frying with reduced salt would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and estimated trans fat (~0.1g/100g), both of which promote joint inflammation; green chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried vadai due to AGE formation and trans fat from the frying process; baking or air-frying is recommended, and green chilli should be omitted if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vadai with black beans (also known as vadai with kacang hitam) is a popular Indian-style deep-fried snack widely available at hawker centres and Indian food stalls across Singapore. It is made from a spiced batter of urad dal (black gram lentil) and wheat flour, enriched with black beans (kacang hitam), spring onion, green chilli, and onion, then deep-fried into a round doughnut-ring shape. The exterior is crispy and golden-brown, while the interior is soft, dense, and slightly chewy, with an earthy, savoury flavour profile punctuated by the heat of green chilli and the sweetness of onion. The addition of black beans increases the dietary fibre and protein content compared to plain vadai. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 219 kcal, 8.38g protein, 12.82g fat, 17.51g carbohydrate, 4.99g dietary fibre, and 651mg sodium. The high fibre and legume base confer a low glycaemic index (~35), making it relatively favourable for blood glucose management, but the high sodium content (651mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and small amounts of trans fat (~0.1g/100g), which are concerns for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Vadai is typically served with coconut chutney or sambar and is consumed as a breakfast item, tea-time snack, or festival food in the Tamil and broader South Indian community in Singapore.

meal_context: side dish with sambar or chutney

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID: F11090066) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on legume-based fritter data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, which slightly reduces overall confidence from the maximum, but the food identity and macronutrient data are fully supported.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific vadai with kacang hitam preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry (F11090066) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g set to 0.1g/100g: estimated from deep-frying process; not directly measured in the HPB source. This value is used for health context assessment (RA trans-fat gate) and is flagged as an estimate. 3. saturated_fat_g set to 1.8g/100g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried legume fritters (urad dal base, vegetable oil frying medium); not directly reported in the HPB source. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on urad dal and mixed legume fritter entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for vadai with kacang hitam was found. 5. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × net carbohydrate per serving (17.51g × 0.80kg serving weight fraction) / 100 ≈ 9.8g per 80g serving. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.38, fat_g 12.82, carbohydrate_g 17.51) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
